subject: ap-chemistry
title: Chemical Equilibrium & Le Chatelier's Principle
stimulus:
A student is studying the properties of CaSO and PbSO . The student has samples of both compounds, which are
white powders.

parts.prompt:
The student tests the electrical conductivity of each solid and observes that neither solid conducts
electricity. Describe the structures of the solids that account for their inability to conduct electricity.
The student places excess CaSO (s ) in a beaker containing 100 mL of water and places excess PbSO (s )in
another beaker containing 100 mL of water. The student stirs the contents of the beakers and then measures the
electrical conductivity of the solution in each beaker. The student observes that the conductivity of the solution
in the beaker containing the CaSO (s ) ishigher than the conductivity of the solution in the beaker containing
the PbSO (s ).

alternatives.correct_example: Ionic solids do not have free-moving ions that are required to carry an electric current. Therefore, there is no conduction of electricity.

parts.prompt:
Which compound is more soluble in water, CaSO (s ) or PbSO (s )? Justify your answer based on the
results of the conductivity test.
Use a pencil or pen with black or dark blue ink only. Do NOT write your name. Do NOT write outside the box.

The left side of the diagram below shows a particulate representation of the contents of the beaker containing
the CaSO (s ) from the solution conductivity experiment.

scoring_points.description: For the correct answer and a valid justification: 1 point

required_elements: For the correct answer and a valid justification: 1 point
alternatives.correct_example: CaSO . The greater electrical conductivity of the CaSO solution relative to the PbSO

parts.prompt:
Draw a particulate representation of PbSO (s )and the ions dissolved in the solution in the beaker on the
right in the diagram. Draw the particles to look like those shown to the right of the beaker. Draw an
appropriate number of dissolved ions relative to the number of dissolved ions in the beaker on the left.
rubric_criteria: For a correct drawing that shows an equal number of cations and anions: 1 point
The drawing shows solid PbSO at the bottom of the beaker (similar to the solid shown for
CaSO ) and fewer dissociated Pb2+ and $SO_2$− ions in the solution.
